speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, just-- we're gonna pick up with-- you have been liberated by the Russians with your group from the bunker. So tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And from there, we came to Tarnow there. And we started to look for work-- something. We didn't have-- I worked in one place for six weeks. They gave me a pair of galoshes. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: As payment. But anyway, we-- we were coming from-- from Russia-- no-- from-- from Tarnow. As we were walking there, there were also very-- with the problem together of a dead people, and sick, and animals. And we came into this place. We were-- we walked into one room, and we were all together. We were afraid to separate. There was a whole house. But we still were living like we would be in the-- in the ghetto-- in hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came back to our city.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Podhajce. And there were a few more families who came back. And then, I met my husband. I knew my husband-- I didn't know my husband. I knew my husband's sister, who was married to my father's cousin. My husband was a family of 12 children. And he was the youngest one. He also came back from hiding. We met in Podhajce. And we got married in Podhajce.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: 20-- 22? How much? 1923-- how much was I?

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] So you got married in 1940-- what day?

speaker: interviewer
text: 1940-- and so how long did you stay in Podhajce for?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we ran away soon, because it was the-- we were afraid to stay there. We were a few months, maybe two months. And we went-- we were-- we were still traveling, coming into to Gleiwitz, to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But it was like-- to Germany still was like pogroms in Katowice, and this. But we were-- luckily, we survived until we came to-- in 1946, we came to Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: To a DP camp. But there was no place that I-- they shall take us in there. And the-- I lived private in-- there was like a Jewish organization, because the camp was fulled-- filled. And they gave me, in the city, a room. I prepared to live in the-- in the camp, but there was no place. So I had to be there. I was so scared for them, for the Germans. My husband start working.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a-- a butcher, making like salami, provisions in the camp, a little.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were there for five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: You told me to rush it.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. You're doing fine. [LAUGHS] You're doing fine. Is there something else you want to tell us about the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The DP camp-- when-- in the DP camp, it was already OK, because I had my apartment. I mean, I-- I had privately in the city. So I was afraid to live alone there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- I took in another couple who came also. And they couldn't go into the DP camp to live. So she was living with me. We shared.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two rooms. So I gave her one room. And one room, I was. And I had my--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my daughter was born, the first one, Sylvia.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1946-- on the way traveling. [LAUGHS] So-- when-- when she was born, we were so lucky to have somebody again who belongs to us, who was our blood and flesh, that we didn't have nobody left over from the family. My husband had one sister who survived with the family. But I was only alone, always. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you want to tell us about how you-- you came in 1951 to America? Did you come to New York? Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came to the United States, we came to the-- no-- to Kansas City, Missouri. And I was there 14 months. And after this, we came to New York. My husband start working right away. And I worked also when my husband came home from work. Oh, my-- my second daughter was born in Bad Reichenhall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that? In the DP camp also?

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In what year? In 1950. When I came to the United States, she was 15 months old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in New York, you-- you and your husband both worked? What did your husband do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband worked in a provision place, where they make salamis and this. And then we went into business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cleaning business, laundry business. And thanks God we-- we did OK. We survived. But it's always haunting me, this. I-- I'm always under pressure. I had a very nice life with my husband, who I lost two years ago. [CRYING] Now I can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: [CRYING] OK. I want to thank you for doing the interview. Is there something you want to add before we end?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know it looks like without a end, but I don't know why. I didn't want to keep you-- kept you--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you're doing fine. Is there anything--

speaker: interviewer
text: --you want to add? [BACKGROUND NOISE] Anything that you could think of that you didn't say, or why you think you survived, or anything like that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why you think that you did, or what you want to leave as a message for people seeing this tape-- anything like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course I want to leave a message-- that it should never happen again, a tragedy like this. [CRYING] Because we are all human, and-- and I don't-- I-- I don't think that it should be such a bloodshed like it was. And only-- I don't know. I don't know think I did something wrong in my life-- until the war when I was a child. The only thing that I had to suffer so much is because I was a Jewish. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this my-- I don't know how to say-- my [NON-ENGLISH]? Do you understand? No, I don't.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is this what I did wrong?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] Thanks, God, that we accomplished a family. I have two wonderful children-- [CRYING] [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: --and five grandchildren. And they all live near you? And we're gonna meet them soon?

speaker: interviewer
text: And they're both married, your daughters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] --don't know. This-- everything would've have to-- to take hours and hours of telling you how it was, even after the-- I mean, when we were liberated. So we suffered so much the way we were. We didn't-- we didn't have where to-- what to do. We-- we didn't know what-- where to turn. We were like-- like wild. We didn't know what. We were all living together in a small room.

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] OK. I'm sorry, no. Continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we started-- they were taking the men then to-- to the army. And we didn't want, because they were all so weak.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was when you were liberated already? This was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. So they-- we starting to-- to do something ourselves. We were working in the bakery, bringing like water to-- for this bakery, so in-- with a wagon.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to pull a barrel of water. So instead, a horse has to pull. So we did it. And we really had a hard life until-- until after the war, until we came to Germany, when the--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the DPs came-- the UN? The UN? Or who was it? The UNRRA.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the Jewish organizations that joined. They were helping us. Then in the DP camps, we start-- my husband started to work. And-- I was raising the children. And that's it. On-- when-- until we came-- I came here when my daughter was 15 months old, and the other one was 5 years old, when we came to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's when you started the business, the cleaning business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And also, my children were always-- I remind myself-- my younger daughter used to come home from school and say, why don't we have a-- a grandma? Why don't we have where to-- telling where to go? The teacher used to tell them for the weekend go to visit your grandparents. And we didn't have nobody. I had nobody. I was left only alone. [CRYING] So-- and with those people that we survived, we made like-- we are not blood relatives, but we made ourselves from-- that this is our-- your uncle. This is your aunt. This is cousins. And we lived very close till today. But unfortunately, it's only a few still left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that's why it's so important that you've told us this.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my children consider them for cousins. And they consider us, too, like families. It binded us so together.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] My-- oh, my headache.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe my children can help me-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --we're gonna bring them in. Thank you again for doing the interview. OK. This is Jean Melzer and some of her family. So, Jean, do you want to introduce us to everyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. This is my daughter, Sylvia, my first daughter! [LAUGHTER] We were very happy when we got her. This is my daughter, Rose, second daughter, which I was also very happy. She's named after my mother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my granddaughter, Stacy, my first granddaughter. And we were in heaven when we had our first granddaughter. This is my grandson, who got married. They got married last year in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is Jennifer, my youngest granddaughter, which I'm very happy to have her, because she's a real doll. Jennifer-- she gives me much pleasure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jennifer's seven years old. ROSE (DAUGHTER): And Stace, and Lori. And Lori-- ROSE (DAUGHTER): And Sylvia's-- Sylvia's next daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: She is 18. And she is now in college, in Boston. But right now she is on a trip, in Europe. And she's not here. I would like to introduce her picture, but later, OK. And then, I have a-- a grandson by my daughter Rose, the first one-- that we had only one grandson. And we very proud and happy to have them-- to have him. And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: Frank. Then, I have another granddaughter, Rose's daughter. And her name is Amanda. She's away in camp. And Frank is away in camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are-- is Amanda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frank is 11. And Amanda is nine. JENNIFER (GRANDDAUGHTER): You said eight!

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Does anyone want to say anything, add anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very lucky to have them. Thanks God that we accomplished to have a family. My daughters are my whole life. And they all very nice. Their husbands are nice to me. And the grandchildren, they're wonderful grandchildren. Thanks God for this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ROSE (DAUGHTER): My mother lived her whole life, basically through her children and her grandchildren, because so much of her own was given up. And everything she did was really for us. And we were really raised with a lot of love. And just-- it was a very special home life that we had and a real closeness that I don't think many families have today.

speaker: interviewee
text: I agree with that. I feel that we were very, very lucky. Both my mother and father were wonderful parents to us. They did everything they could for us. They worked very hard. And they were very, very loving parents to me and my sister, to our children. I-- I believe they were so devoted. I don't think many families see devotion like that. And we were-- we were raised, I think, not just in a loving home, but we-- we were-- we were always given what-- whatever-- they always wanted us to have everything, because they had so little. And they went through so much. They always-- it-- it meant a lot for them to-- to provide us with whatever they could. And I just really wanna say that I feel that we were both very, very lucky to have my mother and father as our parents. ROSE (DAUGHTER): Another thing is even my father, who died two years ago-- my mother, the devotion she took care of him with the seven years he was extremely ill. And anyone else could not have taken such good care of him. Even the nurses that came here and the doctors used to say they've never seen anyone take such care of someone. And I just think that life was pretty unfair to my mother, because she deserved a lot more than what-- Than what she got. ROSE (DAUGHTER): --was dealt to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do any of the grandchildren want to say anything?

speaker: interviewer
text: No? [LAUGHS] How about Lior, who just joined the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: ROSE (DAUGHTER): Lior is from Israel. And he's just joined the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Good family to be in, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. ROSE (DAUGHTER): We're happy that this is being done, because we have always wanted my mother's story to be told.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ask the granddaughter again as we're running along.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to say anything, Stacy?

speaker: interviewee
text: STACY (GRANDDAUGHTER): I just wanted to say that my grandmother is a great woman. She had a very, very hard life. And just like her daughter said-- my mother and my aunt-- she did a lot for us. And my grandfather did also. And they-- they grew up very, you know, with a hard life. And they did so much for us, and-- to not only me, but all of us, the grandchildren. We all appreciate it. I mean they-- they helped us through school and everything. In the beginning when it was hard for us, my grandparents helped send me to Yeshiva. And they aways helped out, not only-- with everything-- emotionally in every way, where they were always there for us. And that's really it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would also like to add that a lot of times I've heard stories from other children of survivors where they say that their home lives were not normal lives and so on because of everything that-- that their parents had gone through. But I-- I think that my parents were both very amazing, because we had-- we had a very normal life. I think we had a wonderful life, and we owe it all to my parents. Even with all the hardships and what they went through, I didn't-- we-- we never had what I've heard other people tell me about their home lives, that were children of survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. Thank you all for coming. We appreciate it. ROSE (DAUGHTER): Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me when I was 13 years old, taken in my home town, Zawalow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- [LAUGHS] In the first row, my brother, Solomon; next, I am; The next is my cousin, Jerome. And the next is also a cousin-- a nephew now, actually, Aaron. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Second line on the left is my cousin, Arthur, and my niece, Genya. She lives in Israel now. The next row is my cousin, Brenda, who was killed. The next one is my mother, Rose-- Rachela. The next one is my aunt, Rose, too. The next one is my aunt, Zala. And the last one is my sister-in-law, [NON-ENGLISH]. Only two of those are alive, me and my niece, Genya, who lives in Israel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On the first row, on the bottom, is my sister-in-law [NON-ENGLISH]. And the second one, I don't know who it is. The third one is my husband's sister. Her name was Chaya. The second row is also my husband's sister, but I don't know the name. The second one is a friend. I don't know her either. And this is my husband's sister, [NON-ENGLISH], and also my husband's sister, Leia. [PAUSES FOR 19 SECONDS] That's the synagogue of Podhajce. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] That's a monument, after the war, that we were taking-- that we were putting up a monument-- my husband and his brother-in-law-- where 1,300 people were killed on the old city in Podhajce. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And by this monument to the right, on the bottom sitting, is my husband, Morris Melzer. And on top, holding the hand on the monument is the brother-in-law, Issac Shortz.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is by the liquidation of Podhajce, when it had to be judenrein. We put-- I don't know how many people there were killed. But they took all those who were left from the ghetto by the last [NON-ENGLISH]. And after the war, my husband and his brother-in-law put up this monument. It was on the [NON-ENGLISH], in Podhajce. This was a picture taken in Bad Reichenhall after the liberation in the DP camp, when he had to get together on [NON-ENGLISH]. This means a-- a-- a prayer that they say after the liquidation of the ghetto, in Podhajce. In the center of this picture is my husband, Morris Melzer.
